results.possibleIdentity: Jan Parker (born 1941) is a British-American artist, illustrator, and book designer who was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s and collaborated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. She is known for her participation in the E.A.T. 'Anand Project' (1969) in India, which sought to develop educational television for rural villages through collaborations between artists and scientists. Her work often involved children and communication, and she has been featured in the journal 'Leonardo' (the international journal for artists and scientists) in relation to E.A.T. She was also a noted illustrator in the 1960s, producing work for figures such as Mary Quant and illustrating children's books.

James C. Parker Jr. was an electrical engineer and scientist at Bell Telephone Laboratories in Holmdel, New Jersey, who was an active member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s. E.A.T. was founded by artists Robert Rauschenberg and Robert Whitman alongside Bell Labs engineers Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer to facilitate collaborations between the artistic and technical communities. Parker is listed in the 1967 E.A.T. membership records as a technical collaborator from Bell Labs, the institution that provided many of the engineers who worked on seminal tech-art projects such as '9 Evenings: Theatre & Engineering' (1966) and the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka. In the context of E.A.T., engineers were often credited as core creative partners alongside artists.

Joan Parker was a dancer and performance artist active in the 1960s New York avant-garde scene, specifically associated with the Judson Dance Theater. She frequently performed in works choreographed by key E.A.T. participants, including Robert Rauschenberg's 'Map Room II' (1965) and Yvonne Rainer's 'Terrain' (1963) and 'Parts of Some Sextets' (1965). Her collaborative work within the Judson circle placed her at the center of the experimental community that led to the formal creation of Experiments in Art and Technology in 1966. She is often listed as a performer alongside other artists like Dorothea Rockburne and Richard Robbins in the performance history records of the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation.

results.possibleIdentity: David Schiller was a multimedia and video artist active in the New York City art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a co-founder of 'Video Exchange' alongside Michael Temmer, based at 500 La Guardia Place. This group was a videotape recording unit that primarily focused on documenting and collaborating with dancers. Schiller was a participant in the circle surrounding the Judson Dance Theatre and was associated with the early video art movement documented in 'Radical Software', a journal that served as a primary network for artists experimenting with new technologies. His work often involved collaborations between art, dance, and technology, which aligns with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that promoted such interdisciplinary partnerships.

David Schiller (also known as Dave Schiller) was a prominent poster artist and promoter active in the late 1960s, particularly in San Jose and San Francisco, California. In 1966, while a graduate student at San Jose State, he organized a series of dance concerts and founded Sparta Graphics. He is widely recognized as a 'pioneer of rock art' for producing and publishing iconic psychedelic posters and album covers for bands such as Jefferson Airplane, The Monkees, The Byrds, and Buffalo Springfield. He frequently collaborated with illustrator Jim Michaelson. Schiller is also well-known for creating detailed pictorial 3-D 'bird's eye view' maps of cities like San Francisco, New York, and London during the late 1960s. He reportedly passed away around 2019.

results.possibleIdentity: Stan VanDerBeek (1927–1984) was a pioneering American multimedia artist, experimental filmmaker, and independent animator active from the 1950s through the early 1980s. He is widely recognized for his early use of digital computers as a creative medium and for his contributions to 'expanded cinema.' In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he produced some of his most significant works, including the 'Poemfield' series (1964–1971), a collection of computer-animated films created in collaboration with Ken Knowlton at Bell Labs. This collaboration was facilitated by the collective Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which aimed to bridge the gap between artists and engineers. VanDerBeek is also known for constructing the 'Movie-Drome' (1963–1966) in Stony Point, New York—a geodesic dome designed as an immersive 'experience machine' for simultaneous film and slide projections. His work often explored themes of global communication and 'culture intercoms,' utilizing then-emerging technologies such as fax machines (for his 1970 'Telephone Mural'), interactive television (for 'Violence Sonata' in 1970), and steam projections. He held significant residencies at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (1969–1971) and NASA (1979), and served as the chair of the Visual Arts Department at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County, until his death.

results.possibleIdentity: Nam June Paik (1932–2006) was a South Korean-born artist widely regarded as the "father of video art." He was highly active during the late 1960s and 1970s, moving to New York City in 1964, where he became a central figure in the avant-garde scene. Paik was a prominent member of the Fluxus movement and is renowned for his innovative use of television sets, video technology, and electronic media. His work often explored the intersection of art, technology, and humanity, humanizing technology through playful and provocative installations. He is closely associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded in 1966 to promote collaborations between artists and engineers; Paik interacted with its founders and participated in major E.A.T. exhibitions, such as "Some More Beginnings" at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968. Notable works from the late 1960s and early 1970s include 'Magnet TV' (1965), 'TV Bra for Living Sculpture' (1969), 'TV Cello' (1971), and 'TV Garden' (1974). He also famously developed the Paik-Abe Video Synthesizer (1969–1972) with engineer Shuya Abe, which allowed for real-time manipulation of video images. Paik is further credited with coining the term "Electronic Superhighway" in 1974 to describe the future of global telecommunications.

results.possibleIdentity: Susan Alexander was a key administrative and collaborative figure associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), the organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, Fred Waldhauer, and Robert Whitman. Based in New York, she served as a staff member and was involved in the organization's efforts to facilitate collaborations between artists and engineers during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Her name appears in historical documents and archives of E.A.T., reflecting her role in the administrative and organizational aspects of the non-profit during its most active period.

Susan Alexander (born 1929) is a New York-born artist who became a prominent figure in the Jamaican art scene after moving to the island in 1951. She was active throughout the 1960s and 1970s as both a visual artist and a dancer. She performed professionally with the Eddy Thomas Dance Company and later focused on painting and sculpture, working with media such as oil, pastel, bronze, and polyform. Alongside her husband, Neville Alexander, she founded the Upstairs Downstairs Gallery in Kingston, which supported young and emerging artists. Her visual work often depicts Jamaican culture and movement, reflecting her background in dance.

Susan Alexander was a dancer, choreographer, and longtime instructor at the Merce Cunningham Studio in New York, active during the late 1960s and 1970s. She was part of the modern dance circle that included Merce Cunningham, John Cage, and Robert Rauschenberg, who were the central figures in the formation of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Alexander taught Cunningham technique classes and was associated with the experimental performance community that frequently collaborated with scientists and engineers on technological art projects. She was later honored for her extensive contributions to the dance community.
